speaker: interviewer
text: Roll four, Stephen Howard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much for-- Why don't you tell me what we were just talking about in the break about where you went after the war and how you found out about your parents, and their names, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in the agricultural school until April of '45. And then I went to Krakow. There was really nothing to do in that little village. In Krakow, I got a room. It was very funny. It was a-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] it was a dormitory of Jesuit priests. [LAUGHS] And I got a job as a librarian. The library was in ruins, and I would go through the books and put them back on shelves and catalog them so that the students could use them. But that gave me just-- well, that gave me board and room. But I didn't have any money, spending money, so I took a job at the barber. And I would be [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] brushing the clients off with a brush after a haircut and clean up. I remember the worst part, which I really disliked, was to clean up the spittoons, but-- [LAUGHS] Anyhow, I worked there for some time. Well, again, I remember my mother's statement, you have to learn, and you have to go to school. So I went to a school following the gymnasium. We called it liceum-- L-I-C-E-U-M. And usually it's a two-year course in Poland. But because of the war experiences and the realizations that everybody was late, they combined this into one year, and we finished two years in one year. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I did go to a Jewish organization, and I signed up in the Jewish organization who  was helping people at that time. And through the Jewish organization, I found out that there were three girls in the classroom who survived. So we became good friends. And I was learning and going through this. OK. I never could find anything about my parents. I mean, it was understood that they had perished because there were a number of people in Poland who my mother knew, I knew. And if she had survived, she would have contacted them. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I-- At one point, I was working in some place, and it was very cold and dumpy, and I was sleeping under a-- a big trunk. Unfortunately, the trunk had nails in top of it, you know, and it was [LAUGHS] terrible. I happened to develop some rheumatism at that time. Because of it, I get terrible headaches and pains.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took me to a hospital. They injected me [INAUDIBLE]. And after several weeks, the pains stopped, but I had to have medication. So I went to a social-- in Poland, there was a social medicine, and I went to one of the places to get medicine. And suddenly, I meet my mother's best friend, Irena Koleczka. She was Jewish, so she survived the war because of her name-- she was married to a Polish man. Her name was Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were amazed--it was just coincidence. She was from Warsaw, too. And she asked me about my mother, and I told her  she didn't survive. And she was very helpful to me from then on-- very helpful. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: After I finished the liceum and got my degree, I looked around, and I said, what am I doing here? I didn't know anybody. Well, the only relative that survived was my cousin, Marisa, in Warsaw, who was the daughter of my aunt-- my mother's sister. And I did get in touch with her in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she was the only one, really, who survived. Oh, I'm sorry-- there was an aunt, another sister of my mother who survived. And she was very old and sickly, and she died shortly thereafter, but natural death. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Many times I have asked questions of, why my mother? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Others survived. Other friends of hers survived. The woman-- remember I told you in Plaszow, in the prison, who told my mother why I had my shirt on. She was with my mother in the thing. She survived the war. I met her in one of those places after the war, and she helped me, too. She immigrated later on to Israel. So one day, the three girls told me that they were leaving Poland. One of them was going to Israel. One was going to France to study medicine. And I don't know where the other-- second-- but two of them were going to Israel. And they wanted me to go to Israel too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I really didn't want to. I didn't really want to go. And I found out-- I remembered that my mother's cousin lived in New York. And I really don't remember how I found her address. Maybe Mrs. Kaleczka knew, Irena Koleczka knew. But I wrote a letter to her. And they said come to join us in the United States. And they filled out papers for me to be able to come to the United States. But the only way to do this was to be a displaced person in Germany. So when the three girls said, we are leaving, I said, I'm going with you. I packed a suitcase and a bag on my back. We went through the Czechoslovakian border. I had to pay money to a guy, and we crossed a river in the middle of the night, where they handled-- my suitcase broke, and the suitcase started going down the river, and I'm going after that. And they said, be quiet. Don't splash the water. We crossed and arrived in Czechoslovakia. They got us on a train. Once we were there, it was arranged by Jewish authorities. We went to Prague, to displaced persons camp for two days, took another train to the German border. This time we went across the mountains to Passau, Germany, where, for the first time, I discovered that anti-Semitism is present in the United States, as well. Because on the border, when one of the Jewish men was caught by the American MPs, the MPs beat him up. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I arrived in Passau, and I met the woman from Plaszow prison, who was there. And I stayed with them for a few marvelous weeks in Germany. And I decided I have to go to the authorities to apply for immigration to the United States. I went to Munich. And I was in the Deutsches Museum. Deutsches Musuem was the place where all the Jews were getting in together, and I had to get another false papers, specifying that I was there before the war ended, which I did. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one day, though, when I was on a street car, getting up I fainted. I was hungry. So they took me to Feldafing, which was a Jewish camp with a hospital. It was a amazing coincidence, because the medical director of the hospital was Professor Rozenhauh, who was a famous Polish eye doctor, who was an uncle of a friend of mine who I met in the ghetto, the Warsaw ghetto, and went to school with him there. It was unbelievable. He was there-- Stephen, his name is also Stephen. And he was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when his father saw me, he immediately got me into the hospital, and I stayed in the hospital for, I don't know, at least a month, where they fed me and brought me up to shape-- to shape. And then I found out that Irena Koleczka, my mother's friend, who spoke perfect English and German, got a job in the Polish consulate in Frankfurt. And she asked me to come to Frankfurt. So I left Munich, and I went to Frankfurt. I got myself an apartment in Frankfurt with a woman there. And again, education-- I started on the university, [NON-ENGLISH] from [NON-ENGLISH], and I started in medicine. I started learning there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, I didn't realize that getting papers would take three years. I applied-- again, I was there in the German-- in the Jewish student organization, and I had to apply for a stipend to a university in the United States. I had to take exams. And, I, of course, passed the exams very well. And they authorized a scholarship at USC for me, here in Los Angeles, where my friend was living at that time-- my mother's cousin was living at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a terrific coincidence, again. But I really didn't pay that much attention to my education in the university because I expected that I will go any week, really. Any week, any month, I was going to leave. I had no idea I was going to take three years.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a job there-- all kinds of jobs, including smuggling money, smuggling American money, traveling all over Germany, under the threat of being arrested any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: For whom were you smugging?

speaker: interviewee
text: Money.

speaker: interviewer
text: For whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: People in the United States were trying to smuggle money to Jews in Germany. There was no way to do this. The-- what they were doing is they were sending foodstuffs, cigarettes, alcohol, other things to Switzerland. They were selling them, and transferring-- for-- for money, for German money. Then they were transferring the money into English dollars and transporting them to Germany and distributing the dollars to the people in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was-- are you understanding it? Selling the things, getting the money, transferring-- bringing the money to the people. And I would be going around, giving the money to the people. With a list-- I had a list-- one list with the names, another list in another post office with the addresses. I had to combine them and deliver the money.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of my most important delivery was to a man named Gieseking, who was one of the most famous pianists-- Walter Gieseking. And I met him and gave him the money. So he was in Wiesbaden. He was next to Frankfurt, not too far from Frankfurt. He lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he survive the war, do you know? I'm just curious.

speaker: interviewee
text: He survived the war, and I heard him play. He was already old. And what bothered me when I heard him play was he was breathing so hard that you could hear his breathing. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually go to his house and deliver the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I would go into his house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he play the piano for you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I heard him at a concept. I went to his concert later on. Well, anyhow, that's my life in Germany. And finally, in November. 1949, I got my visa, got on a ship, went from Hamburg to Boston. Got on a train in Boston to New York, when, by the time my relative, my mother's cousin, was no longer in Los Angeles. She was in New York. And so I went to New York only to find out that they went to Rio de Janeiro. But they left me an address where I could stay. And I spoke just a few words in English. I was looking through the window of the train from Boston to New York, and I said, gee, looks like Europe. There's no difference. The United States is not much different-- forests and land and things and rivers. And that's how I started my life here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in Los Angeles in 1960. As a matter of fact, yesterday was our 35th anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, congratulations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I-- I met my wife at a Polish dance. I joined a Polish folk group-- folk group. And we appeared in different harmonic, and Bovard Auditorium at USC, and we took Polish dances. And at one of the dances, I met my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was married before me. She had one son, Richard, who's 38 right now. And we have a daughter who's 32. She's married now, lives in Woodland Hills.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jackie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you name her for anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: We named her after Jacqueline Kennedy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, what has been your occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am a psychologist. I abandoned medicine when I came to the United States. I really disliked psychiatry in Germany. I felt, at that time at least, they were very mechanical, and I disliked it, the medical approach to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to USC. I told you before, I had a scholarship. And I also got a scholarship later on, and I started in a clinical program in clinical psychology. And I got my-- well, it was difficult, because, again, I was alone. I had to work. So it took me a long time to get my PhD. But I finally got it in 1962. I do want to ask you one question. To take you back-- to take you back to the time during the war-- you were a teenager. How do you feel or what do you feel about those years, besides the great loss of your own parents and everyone? But the loss of those growing-up years that you didn't experience like most people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I thought about it many times that really, I didn't have a childhood. I was working-- I was working since I was 14 years of age. And I was really alone since I was 16, on my own. I many times thought about things which other people in the United States-- my peers here-- have experienced, which I did not.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes they ask me questions, why didn't you do this? Why didn't you do that? And I said, come on, there was a war there. I wish I could have been involved more in sports.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was another thing which my mother always stressed. I would go to a gymnasium-- not the gymnasium school, the gymnasium physical education-- twice a week throughout my-- teenage-- through my childhood. Physical education was very important for my mother. But I never had an opportunity to really do this during the war, and I wished I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about dating, and girls, and all of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dating girls-- ha. My first girlfriend was in the agricultural school. I was 18, and she was 22. And she was a maid to the director of the school, and she became my girlfriend. But I realized after the war that we really have absolutely nothing in common-- absolutely nothing in common. And then I decided to leave Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So once I came to United States, I was very baffled by the American customs. First of all by the fact that you were talking to each other, what we called, informally. In Germany it was [GERMAN], in Poland, too. So this informality was very strange to me. The students at the end of the date would kiss. And kiss in Poland was like becoming engaged. [LAUGHS] So it was-- it was difficult for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I started dating. We went-- well, one of the difficulties, of course, was I didn't have a car. And most of the students there had cars. But we used to go places. I remember going to Hollywood Bowl with a girl in her car and some of the dances at USC. I became a member of the Foreign Students Association, some activities there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's 50 years since the war has ended, is there anything that you'd like to pass on to your children and perhaps grandchildren someday about your experiences and what you've learned you could pass on to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, this may sound strange, and many people would object to this. As a matter of fact, I had a professor at USC who stopped speaking to me after I told her this. Because she asked me whether I hate Germans, and I said no. And she stopped talking to me. I find-- I was in Germany for three years. And I found marvelous people there who were so helpful to me who were helpful to me and-- and sorry for what has happened. And I know they were not Nazis. And I know they were not members of any group. And I found people in Poland, and Jews too, who were horrible to me. I found the Ukrainian who broke my nose. I found the MP from the United States. I found all kinds of people doing horrible things of all kinds of nationalities. And it was my feeling that, given an opportunity, any nation could become like the Germans under special circumstances. And I started differentiating between people and not between nations. And I felt very strongly that there are good people and bad people in every country. That's really what I believe in.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think that brings us to the end. I want to thank you very much for sharing so much of your life with us. Are we going to do the photographs first or his wife? OK, we're going to bring your wife on camera with you first, talk with you for a moment, and then we're going to film the photographs.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, first, do you want to introduce us to this lovely woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife Bibi. We had our 35th anniversary yesterday. Hi. [LAUGHS] We were talking before-- you were asking me questions about my attitudes, about my experiences. And I wanted tell you about a fantasy which I had for years about Mr. Fromer, and the Gestapo man who arrested me and my mother. And I had this fantasy about-- because, you know, so many of the German Gestapo, the SS people, have escaped. They went to Argentina-- they went, others-- which is a horrible thing. We just saw it on My fantasy was that I would be in an restaurant, and I would be sitting there, and I would suddenly see Mr. Fromer with his family sitting at another table. And, as I remember, he came-- and I would go to him from behind, and I would say, hello, Herr Fromer, [SPEAKING GERMAN] Gestapo. And I would call the FBI and have him arrested. That was my fantasy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish it had happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: I bet you do. I know, Mrs. Howard, that you too were uprooted and that you were young. Is there anything you'd like to talk about? I don't want to force you into anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, of course not. Yes, in 1939, Stalin came to that part of Poland which is now Russia, which was where I come from. And someone warned my father, who was a Polish officer, that we were in danger of being deported to Siberia, or wherever. We ran. We fled from there, leaving everything behind-- my father, mother, maternal grandmother, and my two sisters. And we were going towards my grandfather, whom I've never known, my mother's father. We were trying to reach him in Austria, where he was a doctor in-- in a-- a hospital, where my mother was born, as a matter of fact. And we had reached this far-- we took nothing except what we had on our backs. We had reached as far as [NON-ENGLISH]. And the-- the Germans at that time had encroached already in Poland, and we were caught in that net. We were rounded up in cattle wagons. And I recall all kinds of terrible situations. We were rounded up and taken to a [NON-ENGLISH], it was called near Lodz-- L-O-D-Z. And from there, there was a labor segregation, I believe it was called. I really don't know the exact name. And we were taken then, my parents, to various different places of work in Austria. And throughout the war, we were sort of being shunted from-- luckily together. That was our only luck. And so we were together in different places where my parents were working and so on, and survive that way. During the last year off the war, the bombing started, which we welcomed because it was coming from the Americans and from Russians. But we were caught in the chaos and the running away from burning buildings and all the dreadful strafing and stuff. And so finally, luckily that part of Austria was liberated by the Americans. And from then on, the Red Cross took us to Italy. We caught up on our education there-- began to. From Italy, the English-- we when an English camp for a while in Italy. And we were then able to go to England from Italy, continued education and already settled there for five years. And eventually, our dream to come to America did come through. And on a quota, we were allowed to emigrate. In 1951, we arrived here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any Jewish blood in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather on my mother's side, in other words, my mother's father. I never knew him, never met him. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was Jewish, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think, unless either one if you have anything else that you would like to say at this time, I think--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- frankly, I was surprised when I got a telephone call asking me to participate in this. Somebody must have given my name. I never thought of doing it. But frankly, I feel it's a marvelous idea because people are getting older and dying off. And very soon, there will be nobody to tell the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's a living chronicle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, so I hope you're happy. I know it's been difficult, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's been terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But I'm glad I did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you once again. And in just a moment, we're going to film the photographs and your false papers. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Romana Halperin. Her picTURE taken in Warsaw, Poland in 1921, about five years before I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, you have a series of pictures now that are old. How did you manage to save them?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ruins of her apartment in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: You found them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found them after the war. I went to her apartment, the apartment from which she was-- which she left. And I found pictures under ruins, and I picked them up. This is a picture of my mother, Romana Halperin, and my father, Maciej Halperin. They went to a ball in Warsaw, Poland, and it was about 1924. Well, I guess this is the first picture of me, Stephan Halperin, and my mother Romana Halperin, shortly after I was born. And since I was born on January 23, 1926, it must be probably end of January.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city and country?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Warsaw, Poland. And this is a picture of myself, Stephen Halperin, with my father Maciej Halperin, made in Warsaw, Poland. And actually, this was on my birthday-- January 23, 1928. And there's a history to it. My mother was on vacation at the time, and my father didn't know how to comb my hair.
